title: GetX
objectives: Understand GetX via a real-life analogy
See its all-in-one design (state + routing + DI + snackbars)
Recognize why the community is divided on GetX

sections.markdown:
**Think of GetX as a Swiss Army knife with 47 tools.**

- One package gives you **state management, routing, dependency injection, dialogs, snackbars, translations, storage, HTTP** — everything a small app needs, in one API surface. `Get.to(page)`, `Get.snackbar(...)`, `Get.put(controller)`, `.obs`.
- **The upside:** rapid prototyping is FAST. A tutorial-video app that would take 3 hours with Riverpod + go_router + provider takes 1 hour with GetX. Everything is one `.dart` import away.
- **The downside:** you're buying into ONE framework's opinion on everything. GetX also relies heavily on global static state (`Get.find<T>()` walks a global registry). Some patterns fight Flutter's own idioms (`Get.to(page)` bypasses go_router's declarative navigation). Testing is harder because the global registry needs manual setup/teardown.

**The community is split**: fans love the speed, critics point at architectural drift and the maintainer's controversial takes on Flutter idioms. **Best fit:** solo devs on tight deadlines, tutorial/course projects, indie apps. **Not the best fit:** large teams, apps with strict architectural conventions, codebases meant to last 5+ years. Know it exists — you'll meet it in tutorials and freelance projects.

**GetX** is an all-in-one package (`get:` on pub.dev) covering state management, navigation, dependency injection, and utility (snackbars, dialogs, i18n, storage). Reactive state via `.obs` observables + `Obx(() => …)` widgets. Named/imperative routing via `Get.to(page)` / `Get.toNamed('/route')`. DI via `Get.put(controller)` and `Get.find<T>()`. Very concise; controversial architecturally.
## The three GetX pillars

rows: Reactive state that rebuilds on `.value` change
Global registry — access any controller anywhere

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — GetX counter in ~25 lines
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Forgetting `Obx` around reactive UI.** Reading `.value` outside an `Obx` widget → no rebuild. Wrap in `Obx(() => …)` whenever you display an `.obs` value.
**Mistake 2: Using `MaterialApp` instead of `GetMaterialApp`.** Named routes, snackbars, dialogs, and translations all rely on `GetMaterialApp` at the root. Without it, half the API silently no-ops.
**Mistake 3: `Get.find<T>()` before `Get.put<T>()`.** Runtime `Get.find` throws if the controller isn't registered. Use `Get.lazyPut` for lazy or bind via `Bindings` to enforce lifecycle.
**Mistake 4: Mixing GetX navigation with go_router.** Two navigation systems fighting → weird back-stack bugs. Pick ONE per module.
**Mistake 5: Assuming controllers survive process death.** They don't — restart-only state. Use `GetStorage` (part of GetX ecosystem) or wire to Hive for persistence.
## Best practices
**1. Use `Bindings` to organize DI.** Per-route binding classes register controllers just-in-time when the route mounts.
**2. Prefer `Get.lazyPut(() => Controller())`** over eager `Get.put` for controllers that aren't needed on launch.
**3. Restrict `.obs` to view-model fields.** Don't `.obs` every field in every model — over-observability = performance regressions.
**4. If the team is large, don't pick GetX.** Its opinions and global registry hurt maintainability at scale.
**5. Test controllers as plain Dart objects.** `test('count increments', () => expect(CounterController().count.value, 0));` — that part IS clean.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — CounterController.** Build the sample; add reset and decrement.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Two-screen shared state.** Share the same controller between Home and Detail via `Get.find`.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Snackbar + dialog.** `Get.snackbar(...)`, `Get.defaultDialog(...)` from a button, no BuildContext needed.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Bindings pattern.** `HomeBinding extends Bindings` registers `HomeController` when the route mounts; unregisters on pop.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Migrate to Riverpod.** Take a small GetX screen and rewrite in Riverpod. Compare LOC, testability, coupling.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is GetX?**

**A:** GetX is an all-in-one Flutter package covering state management (`.obs` observables + `Obx` widgets), navigation (`Get.to(page)`, `Get.back()`, named routes), dependency injection (`Get.put`, `Get.find`, `Get.lazyPut`), and utility (`Get.snackbar`, `Get.dialog`, i18n, storage) — all under a single import. Concise API, no `BuildContext` required for most operations. **Popular for:** rapid prototyping, tutorial videos, indie/solo projects. **Controversial for:** relying on global static state, some non-idiomatic Flutter patterns, maintainer's disagreements with Flutter team on best practices. Understand it because you'll meet it in tutorials and freelance projects.

**Q2: How does GetX handle state?**

**A:** Two flavors. **Reactive:** wrap a value with `.obs` (`final count = 0.obs;`) to make it an `Rx<int>` observable. Read `count.value` inside an `Obx(() => Text('$count'))` — the widget auto-rebuilds when `.value` changes. **Simple (`GetBuilder`):** an imperative alternative where you call `update()` on the controller to trigger a rebuild of subscribers. Both use a `GetxController` as the container. The reactive `.obs` flavor is more common; `GetBuilder` is used when the team wants explicit rebuild triggers.

**Q3: What's the trade-off with GetX's global registry?**

**A:** `Get.find<CounterController>()` walks a global static registry that `Get.put(CounterController())` populated. **Upside:** no `BuildContext` needed, controllers accessible from anywhere (utility functions, isolates, tests). **Downside:** (1) **hidden coupling** — a screen implicitly depends on a controller registered somewhere else; grep is your only tool. (2) **Testing setup/teardown** — tests must manually `Get.reset()` between cases or state leaks. (3) **Not compile-safe** — `Get.find<Foo>()` before `Get.put<Foo>()` throws at runtime, not build. Modern alternatives (Riverpod) provide DI without the global registry — that's the main structural critique of GetX.

**Q4: GetX vs Riverpod — when to pick which?**

**A:** **GetX** — solo dev, tight deadline, one-package convenience. Fastest to write. Trade-off is architectural drift over time. **Riverpod** — larger team, longer-term app, wants compile-time safety, testability, clean architecture. More setup, worth it. **My rule:** for a new project I know will grow — Riverpod. For a hackathon or a 2-week freelance job — GetX is defensible. If the team is already on GetX, don't rewrite; the migration cost usually dwarfs the benefit. Zealous rewrites cause more bugs than architectural drift does.

**Q5: What are `Bindings` in GetX?**

**A:** A `Bindings` class registers controllers just-in-time when a route mounts (and can dispose them on pop). Usage: `Get.to(() => HomePage(), binding: HomeBinding())`, and `class HomeBinding extends Bindings { void dependencies() { Get.lazyPut(() => HomeController()); }}`. **Purpose:** organize DI per route so controllers aren't all registered globally at startup. This is GetX's answer to the 'when does my controller get created' question — the answer is 'when the binding fires'. Best practice in any non-trivial GetX app; without bindings, you end up with a big `main()` full of `Get.put(...)` calls, which is a maintenance disaster.

**Q6: What are common GetX anti-patterns?**

**A:** **(1) Bypassing declarative navigation.** GetX's `Get.to(page)` conflicts with modern `go_router`. Pick one. **(2) Sharing controllers across unrelated screens** — the global registry makes it easy to accidentally couple things. **(3) `Get.put` at app root for every controller** — bloats startup, retains everything even if unused. Use `Get.lazyPut` + Bindings. **(4) `.obs` on every field** — every reactive value triggers an Obx cycle; over-observability tanks performance. **(5) Testing without `Get.reset()` between cases** — state bleeds across tests. All of these have Riverpod-side equivalents that the framework prevents structurally; in GetX you must self-discipline.

## Real-world usage

- **Solo indie apps + hackathons** — GetX's velocity is hard to beat.
- **Tutorial YouTube channels** — nearly ubiquitous.
- **Freelance projects** — client says 'make it fast' → GetX is defensible.
- **Firebase-heavy CRUD apps** — GetX makes the plumbing quick.
- **Almost never in FAANG / big-team production** — architectural constraints usually win out.

## Advanced notes

- **`GetX<T>` widget** — an alternative to `Obx` that lifecycle-manages the controller.
- **`GetBuilder<T>`** — imperative rebuild via `update()` (not reactive).
- **`Workers`** — react to changes: `ever`, `once`, `debounce`, `interval`.
- **`GetConnect`** — GetX's built-in HTTP client (rarely used vs `dio`).
- **`GetStorage`** — key-value persistence (competes with `hive` and `shared_preferences`).
- **`GetService`** — a controller that survives navigation (a singleton service).
- **`Get.reset()`** — clear the DI registry; essential in tests.
- **`Get.parameters`** — read query params from named routes.
- **`Rx<T>`** — the generic observable; wraps any value.
- **`.value` vs `.call()`** — `count.value` and `count()` are both getters.

flashcards.back: All-in-one Flutter package: state + nav + DI + snackbars + more, in one API.
Global registry: `Get.put(controller)` + `Get.find<T>()` anywhere in the app.
GetMaterialApp (not MaterialApp) — enables nav, snackbar, dialog APIs.
Solo devs, hackathons, tutorial projects, indie apps. Not big teams.
Per-route DI setup — registers controllers when a route mounts, disposes on pop.

options: Global static registry populated by Get.put

Bindings class for a route — registers controller on mount, disposes on pop.
